## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Parole-Eligible Prison Population

In 2020, there were 1,684 people who were eligible for parole but remained incarcerated for a new crime. This group made up 6% of the prison population.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?



``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

People who are Eligible for Parole

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Race ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, Unknown race and ethnicity people constituted the most number of people eligible for parole but still in prison for a new crime, accounting for 58% of the parole-eligible prison population.

``` ::: ::: ### Gender ### Age ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Offense Types for the Parole-Eligible Prison Population

In 2020, there were 423 people who were parole eligible but still in prison for non-violent offenses, accounting for 25% of the parole-eligible prison population in prison for new crimes.

``` ::: :::

## Releases from Prison
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Max-Out vs. Early Releases

In 2020, there were X number of people who maxed out their sentence lengths, meaning they were released on or after their mandatory release year, accounting for X% of releases from prison.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Unconditional vs Conditional Releases

In 2020, there were X number of people who were released conditionally from prison, meaning they were released from incarceration under specific conditions and supervision and often before they served their full sentence, accounting for X% of releases from prison.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

## Disparities
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Descriptive Statistics

``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Race, ethincity, age, and gender

``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Relative Rate Index

The relative rate index (RRI) provides a measure of disparity in outcomes for different racial and ethnic groups by comparing the rates at which individuals of different races and ethnic groups experience incarceration. The rate for each specific racial and ethnic group is computed by dividing the population of interest within that group by the total state population for that particular group.

To calculate the RRI, the rate of each outcome of interest for individuals who are Black non-Hispanic and Hispanic any race, is divided by the rate at which the outcome occurs for individuals who are White. A RRI of 1 indicates no disparity. RRIs greater than 1 indicate the outcome occurs more frequently for individuals who are Black or Hispanic, and an RRI less than 1 indicates an outcome occurs less frequently than for individuals who are White.

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### First Time Offenses ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Compare parole eligibility rates or rates of release to parole for Black, White, and Hispanic individuals with similar first time offenses. For example, are Black people less likely to be released to parole on their parole eligibility date for drug-related convictions compared to White people?
``` ::: ::: ### Time Served ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Compare time served for Black, White, and Hispanic individuals with similar sentence lengths and offenses.
``` ::: ::: ### Sentence Length (not using) ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
